title: Discipline comes first: understanding control in vocational schools in contemporary China
abstract: For the past few decades, vocational education in China has played an important role in the country's manufacturing industry. At the same time however, vocational schools have put more emphasis on disciplinary education. Using a case study of one school, we found that the school had adopted a toolkit of control methods on and off campus. In learning spaces, the school cared more about compliance with classroom discipline than skill acquisition; in living spaces, the school reinforced daily control through the counsellor system; and in thought spaces, the school supervised students' ideology through various tactics. We discovered that these practices have controlled students physically and mentally in a seemingly rough way. We argue that this discipline orientation could be the joint effect of deskilling and intensified political control in recent years.
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
